animalLifeCycles.question: What comes out of a frog's egg?
animalLifeCycles.fact: Frogs hatch from eggs as tadpoles — they look like little fish!

animalLifeCycles.question: What does a caterpillar turn into?
animalLifeCycles.fact: A caterpillar wraps itself in a chrysalis and transforms into a butterfly!

animalLifeCycles.question: What is a baby dog called?
animalLifeCycles.fact: Baby dogs are called puppies. They are born with their eyes closed!

animalLifeCycles.question: Which of these hatches from an egg?
animalLifeCycles.fact: Chickens lay eggs and the chick grows inside before hatching!

animalLifeCycles.question: What is the first stage of a butterfly's life?
animalLifeCycles.fact: A butterfly's life starts as a tiny egg laid on a leaf!

animalLifeCycles.question: A tadpole grows legs and becomes a...
animalLifeCycles.fact: As tadpoles grow, they develop legs and their tail disappears!

animalLifeCycles.question: What is a baby cat called?
animalLifeCycles.fact: Baby cats are called kittens. They need their mum's milk to grow!

animalLifeCycles.question: Which animal goes through metamorphosis?
animalLifeCycles.fact: Metamorphosis means changing shape completely — like a caterpillar into a butterfly!

animalLifeCycles.question: What is a baby horse called?
animalLifeCycles.fact: A baby horse is called a foal. It can walk within hours of being born!

animalLifeCycles.question: What does a chrysalis contain?
options: A caterpillar changing into a butterfly
animalLifeCycles.fact: Inside a chrysalis, the caterpillar's body completely reorganises into a butterfly!

animalLifeCycles.question: A baby sheep is called a...
animalLifeCycles.fact: Baby sheep are called lambs. They are born in spring and love to jump!

animalLifeCycles.question: Which animal lays eggs AND feeds babies with milk?
animalLifeCycles.fact: The platypus is a mammal that lays eggs — one of very few mammals that does!

animalLifeCycles.question: What do most baby birds eat?
animalLifeCycles.fact: Parent birds catch insects and worms to feed their chicks in the nest!

animalLifeCycles.question: How many stages are in a butterfly's life cycle?
animalLifeCycles.fact: Egg → Caterpillar → Chrysalis → Butterfly — four amazing stages!

animalLifeCycles.question: What stage comes after the chrysalis?
animalLifeCycles.fact: After the chrysalis stage, a beautiful butterfly emerges!

animalLifeCycles.question: A baby cow is called a...
animalLifeCycles.fact: Baby cows are called calves. They drink their mother's milk when young!

animalLifeCycles.question: What is a life cycle?
options: The stages of an animal's life from birth to death
animalLifeCycles.fact: A life cycle shows all the stages an animal goes through during its life!

animalLifeCycles.question: Which of these is NOT a stage in a frog's life cycle?
animalLifeCycles.fact: A chrysalis is part of a butterfly's life cycle, not a frog's!

plantParts.question: What part of a plant is underground?
plantParts.fact: Roots grow underground and soak up water and minerals for the plant!

plantParts.question: What do leaves do for a plant?
plantParts.fact: Leaves use sunlight, water and air to make food for the plant — called photosynthesis!

plantParts.question: What does the stem do?
plantParts.fact: The stem acts like a straw, carrying water and nutrients from the roots to the leaves!

plantParts.question: Where does a seed grow?
plantParts.fact: Seeds are planted in soil where they can get water and nutrients to grow!

plantParts.question: What colour are most plant leaves?
plantParts.fact: Leaves are green because of chlorophyll — the special stuff that captures sunlight!

plantParts.question: Which part of the plant makes seeds?
plantParts.fact: Flowers are the reproductive part of a plant — they make seeds to grow new plants!

plantParts.question: What do roots absorb from the soil?
plantParts.fact: Roots have tiny hairs that absorb water and minerals from the soil!

plantParts.question: What do plants need to make their own food?
plantParts.fact: Plants need sunlight, water and carbon dioxide from air to make food by photosynthesis!

plantParts.question: What is photosynthesis?
options: How plants make food from sunlight
plantParts.fact: Photosynthesis is when plants use sunlight, water and air to make their own food — clever!

plantParts.question: Which part of a plant grows towards the sun?
plantParts.fact: Leaves grow towards sunlight so they can capture as much energy as possible!

plantParts.question: What is inside a seed?
options: A tiny plant waiting to grow
plantParts.fact: Every seed contains a tiny baby plant (embryo) with enough food to start growing!

plantParts.question: What do flowers become after they are pollinated?
plantParts.fact: After pollination, flowers develop into fruit containing seeds for new plants!

plantParts.question: Which animals help pollinate flowers?
plantParts.fact: Bees and butterflies carry pollen from flower to flower as they collect nectar!

plantParts.question: Why do plants need water?
options: To make food and stay healthy
plantParts.fact: Water carries nutrients through the plant and is essential for photosynthesis!

plantParts.question: What does a seed need to start growing?
plantParts.fact: A seed needs water, warmth and soil to germinate — start growing!

plantParts.question: Which part of the plant first appears when a seed germinates?
plantParts.fact: The root pushes down into the soil first to anchor the plant and find water!

plantParts.question: What gas do plants release that we breathe?
plantParts.fact: Plants absorb carbon dioxide and release oxygen — the air we breathe! They clean our air!

plantParts.question: What connects the roots to the leaves?
plantParts.fact: The stem is the highway connecting roots to leaves, carrying water and food!

weatherSeasons.question: How many seasons are there in a year?
weatherSeasons.fact: The four seasons are Spring, Summer, Autumn and Winter!

weatherSeasons.question: Which season has the most sunshine and is hottest?
weatherSeasons.fact: Summer is the hottest season — the sun is higher in the sky and days are longest!

weatherSeasons.question: In which season do leaves fall from trees?
weatherSeasons.fact: In Autumn, days get shorter and cooler, and deciduous trees drop their leaves!

weatherSeasons.question: What falls from the sky in winter?
weatherSeasons.fact: In cold winter temperatures, water vapour in clouds freezes into snowflakes!

weatherSeasons.question: What instrument measures temperature?
weatherSeasons.fact: A thermometer measures how hot or cold the air is in degrees!

weatherSeasons.question: What causes rain?
options: Water vapour in clouds cooling and falling
weatherSeasons.fact: When tiny water droplets in clouds join together and get heavy enough, they fall as rain!

weatherSeasons.question: In which season do flowers start to bloom?
weatherSeasons.fact: Spring brings warmer weather and longer days — perfect for flowers to grow!

weatherSeasons.question: What is a thermometer used for?
weatherSeasons.fact: A thermometer tells us the temperature — how warm or cold something is!

weatherSeasons.question: Which type of cloud brings rain?
weatherSeasons.fact: Dark grey cumulonimbus clouds hold lots of water and bring heavy rain!

weatherSeasons.question: What is wind?
weatherSeasons.fact: Wind is air moving from areas of high pressure to low pressure!

weatherSeasons.question: What season comes after Summer?
weatherSeasons.fact: After Summer comes Autumn — days get shorter and temperatures drop!

weatherSeasons.question: What do we call water that falls from clouds?
weatherSeasons.fact: Precipitation is the word for water falling from clouds — rain, snow, sleet or hail!

weatherSeasons.question: Why is it colder in winter?
options: Days are shorter and the sun is lower in the sky
weatherSeasons.fact: In winter, Earth tilts away from the sun — days are shorter and the sun is weaker!

weatherSeasons.question: What creates a rainbow?
weatherSeasons.fact: Sunlight bends when it passes through raindrops, splitting into all the colours!

weatherSeasons.question: What is a weather forecast?
weatherSeasons.fact: A weather forecast predicts what the weather will be like in the coming days!

weatherSeasons.question: What season comes after Winter?
weatherSeasons.fact: After Winter comes Spring — temperatures rise and plants start growing again!

weatherSeasons.question: What is fog?
options: A low cloud near the ground
weatherSeasons.fact: Fog is basically a cloud that forms near the ground — it makes it hard to see!

weatherSeasons.question: Which of these is a type of precipitation?
weatherSeasons.fact: Hail is balls of ice that form in storm clouds and fall to the ground!

statesOfMatter.question: What are the three states of matter?
statesOfMatter.fact: Everything around us is either a solid, liquid or gas — the three states of matter!

statesOfMatter.question: Which state of matter has a fixed shape?
statesOfMatter.fact: Solids keep their shape — a rock stays a rock no matter where you put it!

statesOfMatter.question: What state is water in when it flows?
statesOfMatter.fact: Water is a liquid — it flows and takes the shape of whatever container it's in!

statesOfMatter.question: What happens to water when it freezes?
statesOfMatter.fact: When water gets cold enough (0°C), it freezes into ice — a solid!

statesOfMatter.question: What is steam?
statesOfMatter.fact: Steam is water in its gas state — water becomes steam when it gets very hot!

statesOfMatter.question: What is the process of liquid water turning into gas called?
statesOfMatter.fact: Evaporation is when liquid water heats up and turns into water vapour (gas)!

statesOfMatter.question: What happens to ice when it warms up?
statesOfMatter.fact: Ice melts into liquid water when the temperature rises above 0°C!

statesOfMatter.question: Which of these is a solid?
statesOfMatter.fact: A rock is a solid — it has a fixed shape and size!

statesOfMatter.question: Which of these is a liquid?
statesOfMatter.fact: Orange juice is a liquid — it flows and takes the shape of its container!

statesOfMatter.question: Which of these is a gas?
statesOfMatter.fact: Air is a gas — we can't see it but we can feel it when the wind blows!

statesOfMatter.question: What is melting?
statesOfMatter.fact: Melting is when a solid warms up and becomes a liquid — like ice cream on a hot day!

statesOfMatter.question: What is freezing?
statesOfMatter.fact: Freezing is when a liquid cools down enough to become a solid!

statesOfMatter.question: Can gases be seen?
statesOfMatter.fact: Most gases are invisible, but some have colour — like the coloured gas inside neon signs!

statesOfMatter.question: What shape does a liquid take?
statesOfMatter.fact: Liquids flow and fill the shape of whatever container holds them!

statesOfMatter.question: What does heating do to most solids?
statesOfMatter.fact: Heating most solids causes them to melt into liquids — like butter in a warm pan!

statesOfMatter.question: Water is special because it can be...
statesOfMatter.fact: Water is unique — we see it as ice (solid), water (liquid) and steam (gas) every day!

statesOfMatter.question: What is condensation?
statesOfMatter.fact: Condensation is when water vapour cools and turns into liquid drops — like on a cold glass!

statesOfMatter.question: At what temperature does water freeze?
statesOfMatter.fact: Water freezes at 0 degrees Celsius — below that temperature, liquid water becomes ice!

humanBody.question: How many bones are in the adult human body?
humanBody.fact: Adults have 206 bones! Babies actually have around 300 — some fuse together as we grow!

humanBody.question: What does the heart do?
humanBody.fact: Your heart is a muscle that pumps blood through your body — it beats about 100,000 times a day!

humanBody.question: What organ do we use to breathe?
humanBody.fact: We breathe with our lungs — they take in oxygen and release carbon dioxide!

humanBody.question: What does the brain do?
humanBody.fact: The brain is the control centre of your body — it controls everything you think, feel and do!

humanBody.question: What are our five senses?
humanBody.fact: Our five senses are sight, hearing, touch, smell and taste — they help us understand the world!

humanBody.question: What is the job of our skeleton?
humanBody.fact: Your skeleton supports your body, lets you move and protects organs like your brain and heart!

humanBody.question: What organ digests our food?
humanBody.fact: Your stomach and intestines break down food so your body can absorb nutrients!

humanBody.question: How many senses do humans have?
humanBody.fact: Humans have five main senses: sight, hearing, touch, smell and taste!

humanBody.question: What are muscles for?
humanBody.fact: Muscles pull on bones to make your body move — you have over 600 muscles!

humanBody.question: What carries oxygen around the body?
humanBody.fact: Blood carries oxygen from your lungs to every cell in your body!

humanBody.question: Which organ filters waste from our blood?
humanBody.fact: Kidneys filter your blood and remove waste as urine — they clean your blood 200 times a day!

humanBody.question: What is the largest organ in the human body?
humanBody.fact: Skin is your largest organ — it protects your whole body and helps control temperature!

humanBody.question: What do teeth do?
humanBody.fact: Teeth cut, tear and grind food into smaller pieces so you can swallow and digest it!

humanBody.question: Why do we need sleep?
options: So our body and brain can rest and repair
humanBody.fact: Sleep lets your brain and body repair themselves — children need about 10 hours a night!

humanBody.question: What happens when you exercise?
options: Your heart beats faster and you breathe more
humanBody.fact: Exercise makes your heart and lungs work harder to send more oxygen to your muscles!

humanBody.question: What is the function of the lungs?
options: To take in oxygen and release carbon dioxide
humanBody.fact: Every breath, your lungs take in oxygen your body needs and breathe out carbon dioxide waste!

humanBody.question: Which part of your body helps you balance?
options: Brain and inner ear working together
humanBody.fact: Your brain uses information from your inner ear, eyes and muscles to keep you balanced!

humanBody.question: Why do we need to eat a variety of foods?
options: To get all the nutrients our body needs
humanBody.fact: Different foods give us different nutrients — proteins, vitamins, minerals — that our body needs!

rocksAndSoil.question: What are rocks made of?
rocksAndSoil.fact: Rocks are made of one or more minerals — natural solid materials from the Earth!

rocksAndSoil.question: Which of these is a type of rock?
rocksAndSoil.fact: Granite is an igneous rock — it formed when hot magma cooled and hardened!

rocksAndSoil.question: What is soil made of?
options: Tiny rock pieces, dead plants and animals
rocksAndSoil.fact: Soil is a mixture of tiny rock pieces, dead plants, animals, water and air!

rocksAndSoil.question: What do plants grow in?
rocksAndSoil.fact: Plants grow best in soil — it gives them minerals, water and support for their roots!

rocksAndSoil.question: What are fossils?
options: Remains of ancient animals or plants preserved in rock
rocksAndSoil.fact: Fossils are remains or traces of ancient plants and animals preserved in rock over millions of years!

rocksAndSoil.question: Which rock type formed from cooled lava?
rocksAndSoil.fact: Igneous rocks form when molten rock (magma or lava) cools and hardens!

rocksAndSoil.question: What makes soil good for growing plants?
options: It contains nutrients and holds water
It has no animals in it
rocksAndSoil.fact: Good soil holds water and nutrients that plants need — worms help make soil healthy!

rocksAndSoil.question: What is the hardest natural material?
rocksAndSoil.fact: Diamond is the hardest natural material on Earth — it can scratch any other material!

rocksAndSoil.question: What are pebbles?
rocksAndSoil.fact: Pebbles are small smooth rocks — they get smooth when water tumbles them around for years!

rocksAndSoil.question: Why are worms important for soil?
options: They loosen soil and add nutrients from their waste
rocksAndSoil.fact: Worms burrow through soil making tunnels for air and water, and their waste adds nutrients!

rocksAndSoil.question: What is sand made from?
options: Tiny particles of rock and minerals
rocksAndSoil.fact: Sand is made of tiny particles of rock and minerals worn down over millions of years!

rocksAndSoil.question: Which of these is a property of rocks?
rocksAndSoil.fact: We can sort rocks by colour, texture (rough or smooth) and hardness!

rocksAndSoil.question: Where do most rocks come from originally?
options: From volcanoes and deep inside the Earth
rocksAndSoil.fact: Most rocks originally formed from magma deep inside the Earth or from volcanic eruptions!

rocksAndSoil.question: What is clay?
options: A smooth sticky type of soil that holds water
rocksAndSoil.fact: Clay soil has very fine particles — it's sticky when wet and can be moulded into shapes!

rocksAndSoil.question: How do scientists who study rocks help us?
options: They help find oil, gas and minerals underground
rocksAndSoil.fact: Geologists study rocks to find useful resources like oil, gas, metals and building materials!

rocksAndSoil.question: What is erosion?
options: When wind and water wear away and move rock and soil
rocksAndSoil.fact: Erosion is when wind, water or ice slowly wears away rock and soil and carries it elsewhere!

rocksAndSoil.question: Which of these can you find in soil?
rocksAndSoil.fact: Healthy soil is full of tiny creatures — worms, insects, fungi and billions of bacteria!

rocksAndSoil.question: What is a volcano?
options: A very tall mountain of any kind
An opening in the Earth where hot magma escapes
rocksAndSoil.fact: A volcano is an opening in the Earth's crust where hot melted rock (magma) erupts as lava!

solarSystem.question: What is at the centre of our Solar System?
solarSystem.fact: The Sun is a star at the centre of our Solar System — all the planets orbit around it!

solarSystem.question: How many planets are in our Solar System?
solarSystem.fact: There are 8 planets: Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus and Neptune!

solarSystem.question: Which planet do we live on?
solarSystem.fact: We live on Earth — the third planet from the Sun and the only one known to have life!

solarSystem.question: What is the largest planet in our Solar System?
solarSystem.fact: Jupiter is the biggest planet — it's so large that all other planets could fit inside it!

solarSystem.question: Which planet has beautiful rings around it?
solarSystem.fact: Saturn has spectacular rings made of ice and rock that go all the way around it!

solarSystem.question: What is the Moon?
solarSystem.fact: The Moon is a natural satellite — a large ball of rock that orbits around Earth!

solarSystem.question: Which planet is closest to the Sun?
solarSystem.fact: Mercury is the closest planet to the Sun and moves fastest — a year there is only 88 days!

solarSystem.question: What is a star?
options: A giant ball of hot glowing gas
solarSystem.fact: Stars are enormous balls of hot gas producing light and heat through nuclear reactions!

solarSystem.question: What is our Sun?
solarSystem.fact: Our Sun is a medium-sized star — it looks big because it's the closest star to Earth!

solarSystem.question: How long does it take Earth to go around the Sun?
solarSystem.fact: Earth takes 365 days (one year) to complete its orbit around the Sun!

solarSystem.question: What causes day and night?
solarSystem.fact: Earth spins on its axis — the side facing the Sun has day, the other side has night!

solarSystem.question: Which planet is known as the Red Planet?
solarSystem.fact: Mars is called the Red Planet because its surface is covered in reddish iron oxide (rust)!

solarSystem.question: What is a comet?
options: A ball of ice and rock that orbits the Sun
solarSystem.fact: Comets are balls of ice and dust — as they near the Sun, the ice melts creating a bright tail!

solarSystem.question: Which is the smallest planet?
solarSystem.fact: Mercury is the smallest planet in our Solar System — it's not much bigger than our Moon!

solarSystem.question: What would happen without the Sun's warmth?
options: Earth would freeze and life would not survive
solarSystem.fact: Without the Sun, Earth would freeze at -270°C — all life depends on the Sun's energy!

solarSystem.question: How does the Moon shine?
options: It reflects light from the Sun
solarSystem.fact: The Moon has no light of its own — it reflects sunlight like a giant mirror in space!

solarSystem.question: Which planet has the most moons?
solarSystem.fact: Saturn has over 140 confirmed moons — more than any other planet in our Solar System!

solarSystem.question: What is the Milky Way?
options: Our galaxy — a huge group of stars including our Sun
solarSystem.fact: The Milky Way is our galaxy — a spiral of over 200 billion stars, including our Sun!

foodChains.question: What is a food chain?
options: The order of who eats who in nature
A map of where animals live
A diagram of an animal habitat
foodChains.fact: A food chain shows how energy passes from one living thing to another through eating!

foodChains.question: What do we call animals that only eat plants?
foodChains.fact: Herbivores only eat plants — like rabbits, cows and elephants!

foodChains.question: What do we call animals that only eat meat?
foodChains.fact: Carnivores only eat other animals — like lions, sharks and eagles!

foodChains.question: What do we call animals that eat both plants AND meat?
foodChains.fact: Omnivores eat both plants and animals — like bears, foxes and humans!

foodChains.question: What always starts a food chain?
foodChains.fact: Food chains always start with a plant (producer) — plants get energy from the Sun!

foodChains.question: In the food chain grass → rabbit → fox, what is the rabbit?
foodChains.fact: The rabbit is a consumer — it eats the grass but is also eaten by the fox!

foodChains.question: What is a predator?
options: An animal that only eats plants
An animal that hunts other animals for food
foodChains.fact: A predator is an animal that hunts and eats other animals — like a hawk hunting mice!

foodChains.question: What is prey?
options: An animal that is hunted by predators
A plant at the start of a food chain
foodChains.fact: Prey is an animal that gets hunted and eaten by a predator — like a mouse eaten by an owl!

foodChains.question: In the food chain: leaf → caterpillar → bird → hawk — what does the bird eat?
foodChains.fact: In this food chain, the bird eats caterpillars and is itself eaten by the hawk!

foodChains.question: What are decomposers?
options: Animals at the top of food chains
Organisms that break down dead things
foodChains.fact: Decomposers like fungi and bacteria break down dead plants and animals, returning nutrients to soil!

foodChains.question: What would happen if all plants disappeared?
foodChains.fact: Without plants, herbivores would starve, then carnivores too — all life depends on plants!

foodChains.question: What is a producer in a food chain?
options: A plant that makes its own food
foodChains.fact: Producers are plants — they produce food using sunlight through photosynthesis!

foodChains.question: Which of these is an example of a food chain?
foodChains.fact: Sun → grass → cow → human shows energy passing through a food chain!

foodChains.question: What do we call a network of many food chains linked together?
foodChains.fact: A food web is many food chains linked together — most animals eat and are eaten by several others!

foodChains.question: In a food chain, where does the energy originally come from?
foodChains.fact: All food chain energy starts with the Sun — plants capture sunlight and pass energy through the chain!

foodChains.question: What is an apex predator?
options: The predator at the very top of the food chain with no predators
A predator that only eats plants
foodChains.fact: Apex predators like sharks and lions sit at the top of food chains — nothing hunts them!

foodChains.question: Which of these is a herbivore?
foodChains.fact: Cows are herbivores — they only eat grass and plants!

foodChains.question: What do owls eat?
options: Small animals like mice and voles
foodChains.fact: Owls are carnivores — they hunt small mammals like mice, voles and shrews at night!
